title: Dean-Kawasaki fluctuating hydrodynamics for backscattering hard rods
abstract: We study a system of backscattering hard rods in one dimension. Contrary to the usual ballistic hard rods, these hard rods flip the sign of their velocities with a rate $γ$. This leads to the decay of the odd moments of velocity while preserving the even moments: the number of conserved quantities in the system becomes half. The introduction of the flipping rate, $γ$, is a kind of integrability-breaking perturbation. One expects a change in the transport properties in the system due to the integrability breaking. We show using a Dean-Kawasaki fluctuating hydrodynamic formulation that for $t \gg 1/γ$, the two-time density density correlation spreads in a diffusive manner, and for $t \ll 1/γ$, the correlation spreads ballistically.
summary: This paper explores how breaking the integrability of a 1D hard-rod system via velocity-flipping changes its transport dynamics. By using the Dean-Kawasaki approach, the authors show that the system undergoes a fundamental change in how correlations spread, moving from ballistic to diffusive behavior. This provides a clear example of how a single parameter, the flipping rate, can control the transition between different hydrodynamic regimes. The results are supported by both rigorous mathematical derivation and numerical simulations.
main_problem: Investigating how an integrability-breaking perturbation, specifically velocity-flipping (backscattering), affects the transport properties and the decay of conserved quantities in a one-dimensional system of hard rods.
main_result: The study demonstrates a transition in transport properties where the system exhibits ballistic spreading of density correlations at short time scales (t << 1/gamma) and transitions to diffusive spreading at long time scales (t >> 1/gamma).
method: The authors employ the Dean-Kawasaki fluctuating hydrodynamics framework, mapping the backscattering hard rods to non-interacting run-and-tumble particles, and validate the analytical results using molecular dynamics simulations.
model_or_system: A one-dimensional system of N backscattering hard rods of length 'a' that undergo two-body elastic collisions and experience random velocity flips at a rate gamma.
key_observables: Phase space densities (PSDs), mass density, momentum density, energy density, and two-time density-density correlations.
important_parameters: Flipping rate (gamma), rod length (a), temperature (T), and the time regimes relative to the inverse flipping rate (t << 1/gamma and t >> 1/gamma).
main_assumptions: The flipping process is assumed to be a Poisson process, the system is analyzed near a homogeneous stationary state, and the derivation assumes a limit of small time intervals (dt -> 0).
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the evolution of mass, momentum, and energy densities with theoretical predictions matching MD simulations. Figure 2 compares space-time correlations for low and high backscattering rates, showing the transition from ballistic to diffusive scaling.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the backscattering hard rod model, maps it to run-and-tumble particles, derives the Dean-Kawasaki hydrodynamic equations, provides analytical solutions for density correlations, and validates the theory via molecular dynamics simulations and appendices covering bounded and infinite systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Mass density, q0(x, t), momentum density, q1(x, t) and energy density, q2(x, t)/2 of a system of backscattering hard rods of length a = 1 at different times. The solid lines represents theory and the circles represent the results from molecular dynamics. Here, the initial positions and velocities are drawn from a gaussian distribution (6) with σx = 5 and T = 1. The numerical results have been obtained after averaging over 5 × 106 realizations.

Figure 2: Unequal space-time correlation of mass densities for a hard rod system consisting of N = 400 backscattering hard rods each of length a = 0.01 for a) γ = 0.0005 and b) γ = 2. Here, we have set x′ = 0, t′ = 0. The black dashed lines represent theory (Eq. (23)) and the colored circles represent results from molecular dynamics for different values of τ = t −t′, where we have also subtracted off fluctuations due to finite size effects. Initially, all the hard rods are distributed uniformly in the region [−10, 10] and the velocities were drawn from a gaussian distribution with temperature or variance, T = 1.
